The single import from web-server into web-viewer warrants investigation — server-tier code importing viewer-tier symbols risks eroding the server/viewer build boundary that keeps the two independently deployable
All web package zones except landing-page-assets have test files present in their clusters, indicating consistent test co-location discipline across the package

The task-usage-analytics zone appropriately bundles gateway modules with the analytics services they wire, but this conflation may cause confusion when onboarding contributors who expect gateways to live in a dedicated infrastructure zone
The web-shared → web-viewer → route import chain is correctly directed downward through the dependency stack, matching the zone layering documented in CLAUDE.md

The web-integration-tests zone's boundary-check.test.ts and the monorepo-e2e-test-suite's domain-isolation.test.js together form a two-layer enforcement net for architectural rules — a robust pattern worth preserving as the codebase grows
All five zones in this batch have zero inbound imports from orchestration-tier code, confirming clean separation between infrastructure tests, static assets, and shared utilities

The monorepo test pyramid is clearly stratified: package-level unit tests live inside each package, cross-package integration tests are isolated in the 'integration' zone, and full-system validation lives in the Monorepo E2E Test Suite — a healthy separation of concerns.

Cross-zone import flow shows a strong web-viewer→web-server dependency (33 imports) alongside a bidirectional web↔web-viewer edge (20 + 14), which warrants ongoing gateway discipline to prevent the viewer and server layers from becoming entangled.

The fix→rex-unit (7 imports) and rex-unit→fix (1 import) cross-zone edges form a near-cycle; the reverse edge should be audited to confirm it does not create a real circular dependency at the module level.

Zero cross-zone coupling across all five analyzed zones, combined with the other zones showing mostly inward coupling (web-viewer ↔ web-server), indicates the monorepo boundary strategy is working — package-level isolation is strong.

The fix zone (4 files, cohesion 0.25) with bidirectional imports to rex-unit (fix → rex-unit: 7, rex-unit → fix: 1) stands out as a potential cohesion concern — low cohesion and a circular import pattern suggest these files may not belong in their own zone.

The web-viewer → web-server (33 imports) and web → web-viewer (14 imports) cross-zone flows suggest the viewer/server boundary within the web package carries the highest coupling load in the codebase — this is the area most at risk for unintentional layering violations.

All three domain-tier zones (rex-domain-core, sourcevision-analysis-engine, and the hench Autonomous Agent Engine referenced in context) achieve cohesion ≥ 0.99 — the domain isolation architecture is producing measurable structural benefits across the entire codebase.

The rex-fix-command zone's bidirectional import relationship with rex-domain-core (fix → rex-unit: 7 imports, rex-unit → fix: 1 import) is the only identified circular dependency pattern in these zones; eliminating the single rex-unit → fix reference would restore directional dependency flow.

Static assets and runtime-generated state files (rex-package-assets zone) are detected by the import graph as a separate zero-cohesion community — consider whether .rex/ runtime output directories should be excluded from zone analysis to avoid polluting zone metrics.

Circular dependency between viewer-shell-assets and web-application-core (20 imports inbound, 14 outbound) violates the directed layering principle — one direction should be eliminated to establish a clear dependency hierarchy.

The web-application-core zone at 343 files with 0.96 cohesion is the largest and most internally coherent zone in the codebase, dwarfing all other web zones — sub-zone documentation or explicit internal layering markers would aid navigation.

web-server → web-viewer has 1 cross-zone import while the reverse direction has 33 — the single server-to-viewer import is an anomaly that could indicate a misplaced symbol or an accidental inversion of the server/viewer build boundary.

Static asset zones (viewer-app-shell) will always report zero cohesion/coupling due to binary file opaqueness — consider excluding PNG/HTML from cohesion metrics to avoid false quality alerts in reporting tooling.

The route zone's outbound coupling to web-viewer (5 imports) inverts the expected dependency direction; routing primitives should be consumed by viewer components, not import from them — this is the only directional violation observed across this batch.
Test zones (web-integration-tests, monorepo-e2e-test-suite) uniformly achieve cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0, confirming test files are correctly isolated from production code across the entire project.

All test zones (cross-package-integration-tests, Rex CLI End-to-End Tests, Web Package Integration Tests, Monorepo E2E Test Suite) have zero inbound coupling, confirming test infrastructure is correctly quarantined from production code.

The bidirectional dependency between web-viewer and web (web-viewer→web: 20, web→web-viewer: 14) suggests a potential layering violation; per CLAUDE.md the viewer is built separately and served as static assets, so runtime imports flowing from web into web-viewer should be audited against the gateway rules.
rex-unit→fix (1 import) creates a reverse edge against the expected fix→rex-unit direction; this single import should be verified to ensure it does not represent a domain-layer circular dependency.

packages/web/tests/unit/server/ contains files from 5 zones (data-loading-routes, rex-domain-core, sourcevision-analysis-engine, task-usage-analytics, web-application-core) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

findings.text: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Cohesion of 1.0 across 159 files demonstrates that the hench package has strong internal cohesion — all files are tightly related and serve the autonomous agent's core mission.

The presence of adaptive.ts and review.ts alongside the main agent loop suggests self-evaluation capabilities — these are high-value but high-risk components that deserve dedicated integration tests covering feedback loop termination conditions.

With 159 files in a single zone, hench is the largest single-package zone in this batch; verifying that internal sub-zones (agent, prd, cli, store) have clear directional dependencies prevents internal coupling from growing unchecked.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Coupling of 0 is ideal for a test zone — tests consume production packages as black boxes and are not imported by anything else, preventing test infrastructure from leaking into the production dependency graph.

With only 2 files covering cross-package contracts and scheduler startup, coverage may be thin; consider whether other inter-package boundaries (e.g. hench↔rex, web↔sourcevision) have dedicated contract tests or rely solely on E2E.
Zone contains only test files with no production sources, correctly earning the '-tests' suffix per naming convention.

The presence of a dedicated data-loading-efficiency test alongside the functional route test shows intentional performance regression coverage — a good pattern worth replicating for other route handlers.

The zone is completely disconnected from the rest of the import graph, which means it can be independently deployed or replaced without touching any other zone.

Providing both API-based and CLI-based providers (api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts) gives consumers flexibility between direct API access and subprocess-based invocation — useful for environments with strict network policies.
The auth.ts and config.ts modules at the foundation level mean authentication and configuration logic is centralized — consumers should not re-implement credential handling in higher tiers.
The foundation tier's perfect isolation (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) is the architectural ideal — this package can be updated, versioned, or replaced without touching domain or execution tiers.

CLAUDE.md and CODEX.md coexist at the root, providing AI-readable project instructions; keeping these in sync as the architecture evolves is important to avoid divergent AI guidance.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling indicate the root zone is cleanly separated from all package code — the spawn-only orchestration pattern is working as intended.
The presence of ci.js alongside the package orchestration scripts means CI pipeline logic is versioned with the codebase, which is a good practice for reproducibility.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) across 22+ files confirms the suite is tightly focused on cross-package, full-stack scenarios with no production code mixed in.

The suite covers both structural concerns (architecture-policy, domain-isolation) and behavioural concerns (cli-ci, cli-errors, mcp-transport) — this dual focus is a strong signal of mature test discipline.
Zero coupling to any production zone means the e2e tests exercise the system exclusively through CLI and HTTP boundaries, which correctly mirrors how real consumers use the toolkit.
Isolated files — no import edges between 16 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Maintaining architecture decision records (ADRs) and technical audits alongside source code is a strong practice — it preserves the 'why' behind design choices that are otherwise lost over time.

The viewer-audit.md and process-lifecycle-audit.md documents suggest past investigations into complex subsystems; ensuring these remain current as the codebase evolves prevents stale guidance from misleading future contributors.
With 16 documentation files and zero coupling to source, this zone is purely informational — no risk of documentation imports accidentally becoming runtime dependencies.

No entry points in the e2e zone is expected and correct — test files are executed by the test runner, not imported by other modules.

The zone correctly applies the '-tests' suffix convention: all 9 files are test files with no co-located production source, making it unambiguous that this zone contains no executable application logic.

Both rex-unit → fix (1 import) and fix → rex-unit (7 imports) create a bidirectional cross-zone dependency; consider whether the fix module belongs inside this zone or whether the single rex-unit → fix reference should be removed to make the dependency directional.

Entry points are appropriately coarse-grained (store index, schema index, tree, CLI constants) rather than exposing individual implementation files, which reduces the risk of accidental coupling from external consumers.
The 0.99 cohesion score is among the highest in the codebase, reflecting strong internal coupling discipline — this is a model zone for how domain packages should be structured.

Low cohesion (0.25) and high coupling (0.75) are artifacts of the zone's tiny size — 4-file zones cannot meaningfully satisfy cohesion thresholds, so these scores should not be interpreted as quality signals.
Separating CLI command dispatch (fix.ts in cli/commands/) from core logic (fix.ts in core/) correctly applies the CLI-layer pattern used throughout the rex package.

The bidirectional dependency between fix and rex-domain-core (fix imports 7 symbols from rex-unit; rex-unit imports 1 from fix) suggests the fix module could be folded back into the main rex source tree to eliminate the artificial boundary.

Zero cohesion and coupling scores reflect the absence of any import relationships — this is expected for a zone composed entirely of static data and documentation files, not a structural problem.

Concurrent writes to prd.json from ndx plan and ndx work risk data corruption; no file-locking mechanism is documented — consider adding a lock file or serialization guard for production use.
Runtime state files (prd.json, workflow.md, execution logs) are correctly separated from source code, making them easy to .gitignore selectively or back up independently.
The archive.json file caps at 100 batches and auto-trims, which is a clean approach to bounded state growth without requiring manual maintenance.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Perfect cohesion across 144 files demonstrates that the Louvain algorithm found genuine structural unity — all analyzers, schema types, CLI commands, and tests form one coherent import community with no internal fragmentation.

The package contains its own documentation (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md, README.md) and assets alongside source code; these non-source files inflate the file count but do not affect architectural quality.

The single cross-zone edge (sourcevision → web-server) is the sourcevision MCP server factory being re-exported through web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts — this is architecturally correct and validates the gateway pattern is working as intended.
High cohesion (0.83) — files are tightly interconnected
All service files in this zone have corresponding unit tests co-located in the same community cluster, indicating well-maintained test discipline for the usage analytics subsystem.

Gateway modules (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) are grouped into the analytics zone by the community detector — consider whether they should be treated as a separate 'server infrastructure' zone to make the gateway boundary more visible in tooling.

sourcevision and rex-unit each import 1 file from this zone (per cross-zone imports) — verify these are importing through the gateway modules rather than directly from analytics services, to preserve the gateway pattern.

No cross-zone imports into or out of this zone confirms correct isolation of static assets from application logic.

index.html is the single Vite entry point for the entire viewer build; any script tag or meta change here affects all viewer routes — keep it minimal and delegate bootstrapping to the JS module graph.

Coupling of 0.6 is at the warning threshold: route → web-viewer (5 imports) and route → web (1 import) show that routing logic pulls in viewer internals. Invert this — viewer components should import the route hook, not the other way around.
Splitting the raw route-state module (framework-free) from the use-route-state hook (Preact) is a clean layering pattern that enables fast unit tests without JSDOM.

token-usage-nav.test.ts residing in this zone indicates navigation state awareness is needed for the usage analytics feature — document this coupling explicitly or extract a dedicated nav hook for that feature.
